lessons.title: Rules of Sudoku
lessons.description: Learn the basic rules of Sudoku — what the grid is, what the goal is, and what's allowed.

steps.title: The Grid
steps.description: Sudoku is played on a 9×9 grid, divided into nine 3×3 boxes. Your goal is to fill every empty cell with a number from 1 to 9.

steps.title: The Three Rules
steps.description:
There are three rules:
1. Each row must contain 1-9 with no repeats
2. Each column must contain 1-9 with no repeats
3. Each 3×3 box must contain 1-9 with no repeats
That's it! The puzzle starts with some numbers already filled in (called 'givens'). You fill in the rest.

steps.title: No Repeating Numbers
steps.description: If you place a 5 in a row, you cannot place another 5 in that same row. Same for columns and boxes. This is the core constraint that makes Sudoku a logic puzzle.

steps.title: Try It: Place a Number
steps.description: Look at the highlighted cell. What number can go there? Look at the row, column, and box to figure out what's missing. Click the cell and enter the number.

steps.title: Givens vs User Numbers
steps.description: Numbers that come pre-filled are called 'givens' — they cannot be changed. Numbers you enter are your guesses. In this app, givens are bold and your numbers are shown in a different color.

lessons.title: Scanning Technique
lessons.description: Learn the most fundamental solving technique — scanning rows and columns to find where numbers can go.

steps.title: What is Scanning?
steps.description: Scanning is the process of looking at a row, column, or box and checking which numbers are already present. If 8 of 9 numbers are present, the missing one is obvious. Scanning is how you'll find most of your early solves.

steps.title: Scanning a Row
steps.description: Look at the highlighted row. It has: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9. What's missing? Place it in the empty cell.

steps.title: Scanning a Box
steps.description: Now look at the highlighted 3×3 box. Check which numbers are already in it, and which number from 1-9 is missing. Place it in the highlighted cell.

lessons.title: Naked Singles
lessons.description: When only one number can go in a cell, it's called a 'naked single'. Learn to spot them.

steps.title: What is a Naked Single?
steps.description: A naked single is a cell where only one number is possible. You find it by checking the row, column, and box for that cell — if 8 different numbers are already present across all three, only one number remains.

steps.title: Find the Naked Single
steps.description: Look at the highlighted cell. Check its row, column, and 3×3 box. What's the only number that can go there? Place it!

lessons.title: Hidden Singles
lessons.description: A hidden single is a number that can only go in one cell within a row, column, or box — even if other numbers could go in that cell too.

steps.title: What is a Hidden Single?
steps.description: Unlike a naked single (where the cell has only one candidate), a hidden single is where a NUMBER has only one possible cell. You scan a row/column/box and ask: 'Where can the number 7 go?' If only one cell in that unit can hold 7, that's where it goes.

steps.title: Find the Hidden Single
steps.description: Look at the top-left 3×3 box. Where can the number 7 go? Check each empty cell — does the row or column already contain a 7? Find the only cell where 7 fits.
